questions.question: Explain the difference between internal and external fragmentation with examples.
keyPoints: Internal fragmentation: wasted memory within allocated block due to larger partition than requested (e.g., 10KB process in 16KB partition wastes 6KB)
External fragmentation: free memory scattered in small blocks, total available but not contiguous (e.g., three 2KB holes totalling 6KB but no single 6KB block)

questions.question: Compare First Fit, Best Fit, and Worst Fit memory allocation strategies.
keyPoints: First Fit: fastest, allocates first hole large enough, moderate fragmentation
Best Fit: smallest hole large enough, minimizes wasted space but slower, more external fragmentation
Worst Fit: largest hole, creates larger remaining holes but leaves small holes, inefficient

questions.question: What are the advantages of paging over contiguous memory allocation?
keyPoints: Eliminates external fragmentation completely
Simplifies memory allocation (any page fits any frame)
Provides simple memory protection (per-page basis)
Enables sharing of common code between processes
Supports virtual memory implementation

questions.question: Explain the concept of virtual memory and its benefits.
keyPoints: Virtual memory extends physical memory using disk storage
Programs can be larger than physical RAM
Provides process isolation and protection
Enables efficient multi-programming
Reduces memory contention between processes

questions.question: What is thrashing? What are its causes and effects?
keyPoints: Thrashing: excessive paging activity causing poor system performance
Cause: too many page faults when processes don't have enough frames
Effect: CPU utilization drops as time spent paging exceeds execution time
Solution: reduce degree of multiprogramming or increase physical memory

questions.question: Describe the buddy system of memory allocation with an example.
keyPoints: Divides memory into blocks of size powers of 2
Request satisfied by splitting larger block recursively
Example: 64KB request splits 1MB → 512KB → 256KB → 128KB → 64KB
When freed, adjacent buddies can be combined if both are free
Fast allocation/deallocation but internal fragmentation possible

questions.question: Explain segmentation with its advantages over paging.
keyPoints: Segmentation divides memory into variable-sized segments based on logical divisions
Each segment has base (starting address) and limit (size)
Advantages: reflects program structure, supports variable-sized data, enables protection per segment
Unlike paging: segments have semantic meaning (code, data, stack)

questions.question: Explain the working of paging with a neat diagram. Discuss how virtual addresses are translated to physical addresses.
keyPoints: Paging divides logical memory into pages of fixed size and physical memory into frames of same size
Page table maps virtual page numbers to physical frame numbers
Translation: Given virtual address (VPN, offset), lookup page table for frame number, combine with offset to get physical address
MMU hardware performs translation
TLB caches recent page table entries for fast access
Show example: Virtual address 16-bit with 4-bit page number and 12-bit offset translates to physical address

questions.question: A computer system has 64KB of physical memory with page size of 4KB. If a process has a logical address space of 32KB, answer the following: a) How many pages and frames are there? b) If the page table is as follows: Page 0 → Frame 3, Page 1 → Frame 7, Page 2 → Frame 1, Page 3 → Frame 5, translate the logical address 3500 to physical address.
keyPoints: a) Pages = 32KB/4KB = 8 pages, Frames = 64KB/4KB = 16 frames
b) For logical address 3500: Page number = 3500/4096 = 0, Offset = 3500%4096 = 3500
Page 0 maps to Frame 3, Physical address = (3 × 4096) + 3500 = 12288 + 3500 = 15788
Demonstrate understanding of address translation formula

questions.question: Explain different page replacement algorithms. Which one is most efficient and why?
keyPoints: FIFO: Replace oldest page in memory, simple but may replace frequently used pages
LRU: Replace least recently used page, good approximation of optimal but requires hardware support
Optimal: Replace page not used for longest time in future, theoretical only (requires future knowledge)
LFU: Replace least frequently used page
MRU: Replace most recently used page
LRU is considered most practical efficient algorithm, though FIFO with second chance or clock algorithm is often used

questions.question: Compare contiguous memory allocation with non-contiguous allocation (paging and segmentation). Discuss merits and demerits of each.
keyPoints: Contiguous: Simple, fast access, but suffers from external fragmentation and memory allocation problems
Paging: No external fragmentation, simple allocation, but has internal fragmentation and requires hardware support
Segmentation: Reflects logical structure, supports variable-sized segments, but suffers from external fragmentation
Non-contiguous approaches enable sharing and protection but add complexity and address translation overhead
